Hi, this is Oren and we're now beginning the module O for Offer the Prize. What this module is about is how to introduce the commercial terms of your deal. Think about it this way, we as the pitchers, the pitchmen, pitch women have just invested a large amount of time setting the frame for the audience, right? That's when we take control of the social environment, we raise our own status, we let people know we're the prize, that we're going to be interviewing them to see if they're good customers, that they're going to respect us as sellers and they're going to earn their way into our deal. Then we told the story, right? We gave a compelling pitch, that was 20 minutes or less and then at the end of the pitch, we realized we were running thin on attention, so we used intrigue to reboot the mind of the audience or recapture their attention and that was R revealing the intrigue. So we've been through S set the frame, T tell the story, R reveal the intrigue and now we're going to move into O offer the prize. You see, as much as we want people to understand that they need to be a good customer and well behaved and that we don't want to take counterparty risk, they need to earn their way into our deal, right? Their cash is a commodity, what we have is very valuable. They also need to understand our time is short, our deal horizon is finite and there's a time constraint for them making a decision. We are interviewing them as much as they are interviewing us. The audience needs to prove to us that they're good buyers, they're a good audience, they'd be good partners. But among all this, we still want to close deals, we want to get money, we want to land accounts, right? We're here to pitch and sell ultimately, close deals, get money, land accounts and in order to do that, we need to offer them terms, the price, the delivery schedule, the payment terms, the options, all kinds of commercial terms. For example, if we were going to help you raise money to sell your company, we'd have to tell you what our commission is, what we get for raising the money. So if we raised $5 million for you, we'd have to tell you our price for doing that is 3% or 5%. We'd have to tell you how long it takes and how much cash you would have to put up front. And if I were selling you blue plastic frogs, I'd have to tell you how much they cost by the dozen, where the price breaks are for a container and I have to offer you the extra option of, I don't know, a dozen pink squirrels at $5,000 each to keep the blue frogs safe in winter, whatever, you get my point. So as much as we have framed ourselves as the power player through pricing, as much as we've used frame control to raise our status and our local star power, we now have to offer the price in a moment. We'll cover exactly how to do this. As we move into offer the price, we're going to see some very interesting dynamics and to understand those dynamics, we need to take a look at where we came from, where we are, and where we're going. So to familiarize you with this chart that we're working on here, a lot's going to happen within this diagram. But obviously you can see this goes from the starting point, which is set to frame. It's going to move into tell the story. Next we're going to do reveal the intrigue and then offer the prize, which is where we are now. Beyond that, what you're going to see in the future is nailing the hook point and then of course the objective of all of this, which is getting the deal. So let's clear the screen now. What's interesting and I want to call your attention to as we progress through this, you're going to see some things happening to status. So in set the frame, and you can imagine you walk in the door, get on the phone, get somebody to your website, they open an email, whatever it is, you're starting with very low status. I mean that's the point of all this, right? It's pricing, setting the frame, doing these things is to raise status. So during the setting the frame stage, you are raising status so you can give your pitch from a higher status position. Maybe you do better than this, maybe you do worse, but you definitely are going to see your status raise, your social status raise as you set the frame strongly. Then as you move into giving the pitch, I mean that's going to be your maximum status, right? You give a great story, you set yourself up as an interesting character, you told an amazing story and a pitch about your project or your deal and this is the point where you're at the highest status, right? And that's why we say at the end of the pitch you're able to put counter pressure on your audience, you're able to make them qualify themselves back to you. You're able to ask them why should I let you in my deal? Why should I take you as a customer? And you can do that because here your status is so high, you know a point to it here on the screen, right? At this point your status is so high that you're able to ask those kinds of questions. Why would I take you in the deal? You can see if I fill it in here. This is exactly the point that I'm talking about where it is read here. Okay, but let's move on. So then as you move from telling the story where you've got maximum status, now your people's attention is going to drop a little bit at the end, right? They're tired, you've used their attention for 20 minutes and then you're going to see something like this. When you reveal the intrigue, your status is going to bounce around, right? Because they're tired, you're trying to reboot their attention, something hard to do, but to degree you succeed, you'll get your status back so it will drop high and go low again, it will bounce around. Then and here's what I really want to call your attention to. In this module where now offer the prize, it's really going to drop and it may drop lower than this because you're now offering the deal, you're asking them to sign up for something, you're telling them commercial terms, you're giving them the price and you're going to see your status drop when you try and get resources from someone. If you think about it, this ties back in some ways to our ancient brain or ancient mind, right? A hundred thousand years ago when one human walked up to another human and said, I want something that you have created fear because resources were so constrained in those times that anybody trying to get your resources causes fear inside you. So in the same way, when you are starting to act needy and you're going to act a little bit needy because you need to sell them this product, this idea, this project. When you start to act needy, offer the prize, make an offer, give the price, you're going to see your status drop. And then what's good is when you start to nail the hook point, which is the module after this, it's really your status is going to go way back up and that's going to be a great time. So one way to visualize this would be maybe to look at this wave here. In the beginning, you're going to come with low status, it's going to accelerate and you're going to end the frame setting period with pretty high status and you're going to give your pitch, it's really going to climb up high. This is going to be your peak status right here and that is when you're going to apply the counter pressure and say, why would I ever let you in my deal with everything that I have going without knowing more about you, right? Then you're going to burnt out their attention and really have done a lot of emotional things and your status is going to drop. It might drop precipitously like I've shown here, but you're going to reveal some intrigue and get it to climb back up. That's the job of intrigue is to get attention and status back. Then in the module we're in, we're going to see status drop again because we are giving commercial terms and price. We're starting to ask for stuff and then in nail the hook point, it's going to waver around a little bit. So if I overlay all of this, you can see how it looks. What I love about this worksheet and looking at it in this format is you can really see how the modules build on each other and how they interplay with status. So let's put the status line back up. Then let's look at the modules by name, set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue. Let's just leave those three up and then let's go ahead and look at the elements within set the frame. So now if I put the arrow over here, you can see of course we've discussed many times pricing the sense of walking in the room with no status, no frame control, nothing. The other person, the buyer, the guy who makes the decisions having all the power. And then converting the power frame over to us by saying we're in control of this meeting, we're in control of the agenda, you're the buyer, all you have is money, we have this product, this idea, this venture. It's incredibly valuable and we could work with anybody, we want to choose you but of course we're going to have to hear about you so we're going to give our presentation. All those elements surprising that we've talked about. We're certainly going to give the big idea. We've talked about the big idea many times, right? If you could coat ball bearings with Teflon and Kevlar and really slippery oil which has never been done before, you can triple the speed at which machinery works and if you could do that you could grow a billion dollar company in just the United States domestic market in under five years and that's what we're here to show you today, the possibility of doing that. So you're going to give the big idea. This is all under frame setting. While you're doing that you're going to be establishing frame control. As you establish frame control, your status is going to be reaching new heights and right when your status is as high as can get in the frame control segment then you're going to move on. You're going to have maximum attention. You're going to have maximum attention when it's time, very early in the meeting. You're going to move into your pitch. So let's see what that looks like. Within the pitch of course you're going to do these things that we've talked about over and over. Build a compelling character. Give the big idea. Really spend some time deepening the problem, making it painful, making sure it's understood how the range of this problem and how real human beings have this very deep problem and it causes problems in their lives. And then you're going to say, hey, we're not the smartest, greatest, best guys on earth. Other people can solve this. But we have a solution that's pretty damn good. Better than all the other stuff out there, that's for sure, and we'll show you how it works. So that would be a good 20 minute pitch. Of course there's other stuff to do in the pitch, but if you did just that you'd be quite successful. And then we know your status here is going to be very high if you've done a great pitch and people are going to start acting weird. They're going to try and status correct because you've raised your status so high by giving a structured, interesting, compelling, funny, exciting pitch narrative that includes human stories because you've done all this. The audience is going to want to give their own stories with that same great narrative, but they don't know how to do it. They don't know the underlying structure. And so they're going to be weird about it. They're going to ask you questions to try and lower your status. So they might say, well, we heard that the Teflon wears off the ball bearings within a year. Or many other people have tried to build that kind of company and have failed. So they're going to try and ask you those questions. Explain yourself, but you're not going to chase those conversation threads. But now you know you've kept somebody's attention for over 20 minutes and they're fatigued. So now we're going to have to reboot their mind with intrigue. And here's where before we can continue and really offer the prize, which is the module we're in now, we've got to reveal some more intrigue. Status is going to be moving all over the map. That's certainly going to be dropping just because people's attention is waning. So we're going to remember the three principles of the human condition, right? Well, of course, we know that humans have three emotions, angry, hungry and horny. But really how that plays out is we want that which we cannot have. We chase that which moves away from us. And we only value those things in which we pay for them, right? So when we deploy some intrigue, it's going to take advantage of those things. You can't have it. You have to apply for it. And there's more to our product that you haven't seen. You can't have the premium product. So you're going to really provide some intrigue at that point. And after intrigue, there's the module we're in now, which is offer the prize. And offering the prize is really hard. That's why status is going to drop because now we want something. Now we want something from the other people. And so we're going to be talking about price and terms and contracts and really moving product out of our factory or out of our servers into the customers, making them pay for it on a timeline. And there's a process for buying. There's all hard stuff to talk about because this is where really a transaction is going to happen around terms and price. That's offering the prize. Then after that, we're going to nail the hook point, which is really all about calibration. So and we'll get to that next. I want to just see you want you to see this build one more time and see how much you've accomplished in such a short time. So we're going to put the status build back in there up through the highest point and tell the story. We're moving to reveal an intrigue. It's going to bounce around and then in offer the prize, you're really going to be in a low. You got to work hard because you're going to be in that low status period. And let's watch what happens. We build, set the frame, all those things, pricing, the big idea, time constraints, getting attention, getting the room or the call under frame control. I'm going to leave it up there and watch what happens. Now T, you tell the story, give the pitch across 20 minutes, be prepared for the questions that are going to come at you. You're going to build your character, really deliver the problem, explain the solution, how it works. You're going to reveal intrigue right after that and look how much you've accomplished and how much you're doing. So it's pretty amazing to see it all build across time. Then we're going to be frame stacking and then move into nearly the hook point and getting the deal. And this is the pitch process from start to finish. But don't get overwhelmed by this page. You're just going to break it down into whatever module you're in and take it piece by piece. So again, if you're in just giving the pitch, this is all you have to deal with. Building the character, explaining the problem, giving your solution how it works. So don't get overwhelmed by this whole map of things you have to do within each module. You know exactly what to do. Okay, now let's get past the schematic here and really get into the steps we have to take to offer the prize. So far, I have showed you the first three phases or modules of the pitch. Setting the frame, telling the story, revealing the intrigue. And so by now, you've held the audience or the buyer's attention for a while. The buyer, the audience knows the essentials of what you have. Who you are. Why you're a great character. Why this idea is important and how it works. He knows what your value proposition is. And he gets exactly what the big idea is. He knows when he buys what he's going to get. But of course, in a great pitch, you have to do more than just show and tell. This is a sale. This is a business development. We're here to make a deal happen, to make money transact. So now towards the end of the pitch, you only have a few minutes left to propose something concrete and actionable. A sale, a transaction, a deal, terms. You have to propose price. The delivery schedule. The installation, however it works in your business. Minimum order volume. How the transaction works. So welcome to the next phase, right? Offering the prize. Offer the terms in a way that's hot. Offer the terms in a way that's hot. And I call that a hot cognition. So let's go through now exactly what a hot cognition is and then how to offer the terms in a hot cognition. Part of this is revisiting the ideas of frame control and the idea that the pitch is done in a social way, in a social environment. And get frame control and your perspective becomes the dominant perspective. Right? Because the audience or the buyer can like your deal or be afraid of it before he or she knows even much detail about it. And he can probably decide yes or no without even knowing exactly what it is. That is a hot cognition. That's what hot cognitions do. Deciding something you like before you fully understand it, that is a hot cognition. And that is why in our pitch we don't go into much detail. And when we get the analytical questions, we defer them to later because we want this to be understood and we want it to be felt by the audience in a hot way, not in cold reasoned analysis, but in a hot cognition. Think about it in terms of your own life. What do you do? Research, analyze, and then decide. Let's say you're going to buy a car. Step one, research. Step two, analyze your research. Step three, decide which one you're going to get. And so look, if we were all computer-like or even behaved like economists think we do, it would work this way, but we are not analytical by nature. It doesn't work like that. Research, analyze, decide. Right? Sometimes we think we've thought it through or we use the decision matrix. Right? We think we're smart, careful, and rational decision makers, but really we buy what we want. In decision making, we don't do much analysis, if any at all. We go with our gut. You know, Jack Welch from GE, we all know the famous manager who really built a huge amount of value into that company. And when he wrote his biography, as I wrote in my book, as I wrote about him, he didn't call it intense analysis by Jack Welch. Right? It was called straight from the gut. There's a whole side to us as humans that the computers don't have. Right? Our bodies and our minds, we just know in our gut, that's what we call it. We know the situations that we meet in life and how we should respond and what we want. Solvences are hot cognitions. You know the car that you want. If your research and your analysis and your decisions is that you should get a station wagon, but you go see Volvo's new sport wagon, you immediately throw all the research out and you go, that's what I want. I'm paying for it. That's what my gut tells me to get. I say it in this way because this is what is important to create in the audience, this feeling of wanting. This idea of wanting is such an interesting subject to me and I hope to you as well. Think about you and your friends. You didn't become friends with each other because you did some kind of analysis. You didn't choose your career because you analyzed what in 10 years was going to be the highest paying career and you don't choose what sports you're going to watch out of some kind of detailed analysis, the pros and cons of each sporting situation. If you really stop to think about it, most major decisions in your own life are not made by cold, reasoned and analytical cognitive processes. You don't do evaluation and analysis, but instead the sports you watch, the people you love, the friends that you have, those are hot cognitions, probably the car you drive, the city you live in. That's what you wanted. There are probably very few decisions in your own life that aren't hot, even stuff that you should be thoughtful about, like what computer you should buy. I've got this MacBook Pro here and I love this machine, but probably the PC that I switched out of is a better computer, but damn, this MacBook Pro is beautiful and I just wanted it when I saw it. I'm sure in your own life, if you think about it, very few decisions that you made analytically those are hot cognitions. I say it in this way so you know what to recognize when I say a hot cognition, because now we're going to move it out of your personal experience into your business life and your business relationships. Even in your business relationships, most of the time, the data you have collected about the choices and alternatives and the options that you use to buy a copier or to lease some office space or get some fleet trucks. You didn't use those analytics to make the decision anyway. You use them to justify decisions after the fact. As I said before, we buy the cars we like, we lease the office space that we like or want, we choose employees, even all kinds of analysis, we choose the ones we like, maybe any of the ones we find attractive from a personality standpoint. Then we justify these choices to other people with all the facts and explanations and rational analysis that we did. We buy what we want, the copier, the lease, the fleet car, the CFO, the employee we want, and then we rationalize those choices with the research we did. We don't need facts and explanations to convince ourselves. We know what we'd like, even when we try the rational approach of making lists, pros and cons. This is my summary point. When we try the rational approach and we make those lists and we do the spreadsheets and we do the analysis, if it does not come out how we like, we go back and we redo the analysis until it comes out the way we want it. That is hot cognition. Ultimately, if we want to create that hot cognition now, when we offer the price in the terms of our deal, we want to create hot cognition in the buyer, the audience, how do we do that? We're going to reveal the terms of the deal within a four frame hot cognition stack. We're going to reveal the terms of the deal within a four frame hot cognition stack. To make it work, you just implement the frames you're already familiar with from the set the frame module. All you have to do is learn how to stack them up one after the other to generate this hot cognition. In other words, to create what I call wanting. We're not trying to get the buyer to like us because learning to like something is slow and it's intellectual and it requires analysis and it all happens up in the neocortex. This is not the business I'm in, getting people to like me or getting people to like my deal. I want high temperature frames that create hot cognitions that create wanting, hot wanting. I use frame stacks to make sure that the mind and the brain of the person that I'm pitching to wants what I have, wants to work with me, moves towards me, ends up chasing me in a hot cognition in order to get the deal. Here the four frames we're going to stack in quick succession to introduce the terms of the deal. If you do this correctly, by the way, you're going to easily move into the last module, the last part of the pitch, the hook point. The four frames and you can use many more. These are the four that I've found very effective. The first frame in your frame stack that will help you create the hot cognition is the intrigue frame. I'm going to show you in a moment how to use the intrigue frame to introduce price. The second will be the prize frame. We'll use the prize frame to introduce terms. The third will be the time frame. I will use in the example the time frame to talk about minimum order volumes, introduce the term of minimum order volume. Then the fourth will be the moral authority frame. I'm going to use the stack in the fourth, the moral authority frame to tie it all together and complete my four frame stack. Of course, the hardest part of making a sale closing the deal is introducing price because everybody wants to pay a lower price. I might say it like this. John, the price is more than you want to pay, of course. How could it ever be different? It's less than we want to charge. The blue plastic frogs, they're $1,000 each. There's no way around that. They're actually $1,001. That's just the price. It's super interesting. You are going to meet with the order size that you have to place, for the size of fields that you have to put these frogs in, for your insect defense system. Because of the size of order you have, you're going to meet our chief engineer. This guy is amazing. He's the guy that picks the blue. He flies to Afghanistan twice a year and goes out with a military escort to those areas because Afghanistan, and this is, look it up, is the only place in the world where real crystalline blue grows naturally and volume enough to create this blue color. If you grow this blue color in the lab, it doesn't come out right. The insects aren't afraid of it. But look, you've got to meet the engineer. This guy's going to be your instant friend. When he describes the manufacturing process, it is so amazing. Most fascinating thing you'll ever learn. Anyway, with the first delivery, I'm going to bring him along. He's going to help with the install. You're going to love the engineering team, and specifically Rolf, who is the lead engineer, and he's the one that sources the blue. The intrigue frame can be done just as simply as that. Even without pause, we'll move into the prize frame. As you know, with this order of blue plastic frogs, you need to get an irrigation report, and these are $25,000. Fortunately, in my previous life, I was an irrigation consultant, and I have all the models. I still have the license. They're kind of time consuming to do these irrigation reports. Although I signed maybe 20 or 30 new customers a month, I only do five irrigation reports. I've already done four this month, because they're just time consuming. They're extremely valuable. It's probably going to cost you $25,000 to get one. But I think, and if I look in my order book and I remember this stuff, you're going to be my fifth customer, and I'm going to do the irrigation report for you. I'm going to do this for very few people. I don't really care about the money, but if you want to work with me, this is the kind of stuff I bring to the table. When you buy this order from blue plastic frog industries, what you get is me. You're going to find that's incredibly valuable both economically and also as a resource for your business. You're going to get that, and we're going to do that together. Then of course, I'll move without pause into the time frame. To the time frame. The blue plastic frogs are funny, especially this time of year, because this is when our inventories go low. Look, this isn't me. It's not a tactic. The French buy these things up. I don't know why, but this time of year, inventories get below $10,000. They are dropping low. When it gets below $10,000, there's a price trigger, and the price goes up 33%. There's nothing I can do about it at that point. I'm just telling you right now, you have about a week to finalize this order, or price increase is going to get triggered. You're not going to be able to come crying to me and saying, I want the previous price. That is the real time frame. I'm not saying you have to buy today or tomorrow, but I am saying there is a realistic time frame in which this deal has to get done, or both going to be singing the blues about the price. Then next, of course, we'll move into the moral authority frame without pause. This will be the fourth in our four frame stack. I think it could go something like this. The frogs, the blue plastic frogs that we manufacture, they're the best in the world as we've been talking about. They're five gold star, EEE, lead rating. They're the highest environmental rating in the world. Of all irrigation and insect deterrent manufacturers, we have the highest environmental manufacturing and highest environmental quality rating. One problem is we find companies like you buy our product in order to demonstrate to regulators that you're environmentally friendly. We don't like you using us as a veneer or patina or a casual demonstration of environmental friendliness. We got to make sure that you guys actually are environmentally friendly throughout, and we're not your only environmentally friendly effort. You have to prove to us what other environmental programs you have running before we let you place an order of this size because it's important to us that our customers are real and care about the environment, and they don't just use a small order with us or a large order with us to frame themselves as they're environmentally friendly. They really have to be that way throughout the organization, and that's something we're going to need you to prove to us. That would be a good example of the moral authority frame. Now we've delivered the intrigue frame around price, the prize frame, around terms, the time frame when the decision has to be made, and the moral authority frame to tie it all together. I don't think you have to overthink this four frame hot cognition stack, the four frame stack, because you've done everything else so well up to this point. You can actually do it quite calmly. Hey, yes, the price is $1,000 each, but guess what? As part of this, you're going to be meeting the engineers and these guys travel to Afghanistan and they buy this stuff and they have these incredible adventures, you're going to start to meet this team. You're certainly going to get to work with me, and I'm unlike the other sales reps of irrigation and insect deterrent equipment. I'm an irrigation consultant, I add tons of value, I care about my customers, and I'm probably the most knowledgeable rep in the world about this product and how to deter insects. That's something that I'm really glad you took the effort to convince me to sell these to you because we're going to get to work together, you're getting a lot in this transaction. By the way, no, there's no free delivery and there's no free installation, but you do get to work with me and that's a huge benefit. Look at the time frame, you don't have to decide today, I never want anybody to decide in a single day, but I'm telling you right now, the French buy this stuff this time of year, stock levels drop, the price goes up. If you ask me, and I've been doing this for 10 years, if you don't want to get a 33% price kick in the summer season, you have about a week to decide. The last thing is, you have to be honest about your intent with these things. We know we have the best environmental rating in the world. It's one of our features, it's one of the reasons that it's about $1,000, maybe that's a little bit more than an ex-guys, but you cannot and we will not allow you. You have to tell me right now if this is your intent, we won't allow you to buy these things to show to regulators that you're environmentally friendly if the rest of your program is in triple E or four E rated and if you're not trying to get your lead accreditation for environmental friendliness. You see the four frame stack can be done in a fairly calm way and you'll find frames of your own and add them in, but it's important to put them one after the other and that's when you're introduced to price, the terms, the order volume and all those kinds of things. Let's talk a little bit more about hot versus cold cognition. I want to revisit that for another minute. As I once wrote, maybe the best way to define a hot versus cold cognition is to compare it to chocolate and spinach, right? Because you know the cold hard facts. Spinach is good for you and has a lot of nutrients and you should be eating more of it. I'm telling you right now you should be eating more of it. I should, but when you or I are offered a piece of chocolate instead, we go for it, right? That's the difference between hot and cold cognition. We know spinach is good for us, but we go for the chocolate and that's the test of whether your pitch goes well, right? Does the audience or the buyer, do they want it? Do they want to buy it? Do they want to be involved with you? Do they want to invest in you and your idea and be your partner? How much thinking about your presentation does your audience need before he or she forms a preference about it? How fully and completely must an idea be presented and thought about? And how much rational analysis needs to be completed before the audience decides good or bad? First of your pitch right now, you've done a great job of this. You've focused on emotion and characters and you've got structure and it's structured around problem and solution. And when analytical questions have come at you, you've deferred them. So you've done a good job at really creating hot cognitions, but now in offer the prize, you're giving terms and price and there's some risk. You don't want to wait for an evaluation of the price or your target, your buyer will wander off into cold cognition, cold cognition and think about the terms and start to evaluate. So by stacking the four frames and triggering hot cognitions, you create a more instantaneous evaluation and the reaction of wanting. Next a couple more thoughts about hot cognition. Hot cognition is the inner certainty of knowing something and by feeling it. A cold cognition is a certainty of knowing something by evaluating it. But hot cognitions are extremely fast. Hot cognitions develop through these ancient brain structures through the stem and the midbrain, near the crocodile brain. Cold cognitions are slow. They're analytical. They develop in the neocortex. Cold cognitions are calculated and they take time to suggest a solution. This is how the neocortex goes about its business. It collects and aggregates information over time and it solves problems. You've heard the expression, just give me the cold hard facts. That is what is meant by cold cognition, the labor intensive process of combing through facts and building a decision matrix. You can trigger a hot cognition instantly. You can trigger a wanting even though you're delivering the price. Cold cognition analysis can take hours or days and most presentations, especially at the point where they deliver price are set up to take the audience down the path of cold cognition to try and justify the price with facts and information. So look, let's summarize it like this. It's really basic. You can achieve a hot cognition around price and delivery terms in your audience by stacking four frames quickly, one after the other. And you can help that your audience, your buyer in his mind achieve wanting even through the price and the terms, the commercial terms section of your pitch or your closing. They can still have that feeling of wanting if you stack those frames and create that hot cognition. Now that we've talked quite a bit about why hot cognition is important and how in theory to stack four frames to get that hot cognition, let's talk more specifically about how to do it in your business. So I think there's five steps. So first of all, write your timeframe, your intrigue frame, your prize frame, and your moral authority frame. So write each of those on an index card and make sure to note on each one which of the commercial terms you're going to use in that frame. So for example, for me in the timeframe, I use that to deliver the, I mean clearly the order date. Okay, that's an easy one. The timeframe you will use to deliver the final order date or if you're closing a deal. So the deal has to be closed in, you know, by June 30th, October 15th, whatever it is. So that will be your timeframe. Your moral authority frame. Only you will know what that is in your business. Right? Usually I'll use the moral authority frame to tie it all together. The intrigue frame, the intrigue frame can be very good for delivering the price. Okay? The price is $1,000. The price is $100,000. The price is $5 million. Very interesting about this is XYZ. Okay? They use the intrigue frame to deliver price. It's very useful. The price frame, the price frame can be used to deliver lots of commercial terms. Okay? Such as the minimum order volume. Okay? I only work with people who order 100,000 crates of ball bearings or more because I've been doing this for 10 years and I work with big customers. Okay? Easy. Price frame around order volume. So timeframe, moral authority frame, intrigue frame, price frame. Those will be the four that you stack. Just write them on an index card. Step two will be practice. All right? You've got to flip through those cards and deliver pretty smoothly, you know, because you're delivering a lot of information and commercial terms all at once. So make sure you can go through that whole four frame stack smoothly in a way that creates wanting. So it's practice, going through it, having the cards, going through it, and practice and making sure you're smooth. Now step three, step three is going to surprise you because it's so easy. After you've delivered the four frame stack, all right, it's time to do the following. You've draw. Get out of there. All right? You have done everything you can do and you need to start moving backwards. I think I've said it three or four times already in just this module. People chase that which moves away from them. We want that which we cannot have and we only value those things that we work for. You've done as much as you can possibly do. Do not lead in and be needy. Start with drawing. I'm going to give you the next step away to do that. In step three, you're going to start with drawing. Literally start moving backwards. Step four is to leave the room. Now bring the guy with you or bring the audience with you, but go somewhere else. That's called a location shift. It is now time and there's many, many reasons beyond the scope of what we can get into here. It is now time to do a location shift. Withdraw and then leave the room and take them. If you can get them to tour the factory or go see the sunshine outside or go do to the break room or go get a cup of coffee or go out to lunch or whatever it is. Withdraw. You can keep them with you and you can continue talking about the deal, but outside of that pitching environment. I cannot emphasize this enough. Create a location shift. Withdraw and then the final step is to leave. Step five in this process is to remember that you still have to nail the hook point. You're going to move smoothly into the next module. That's what we'll be doing next. Go through the workbook, obviously, and offer the prize. These steps will be laid out. Get your index cards going with your time frame, your moral authority frame, your entry frame, and your prize frame. Get good at delivering that smoothly. When you're done, remember to withdraw. Then move into a location shift and you've done it. You accomplish that. You've done it as perfectly as you've done it.
